title: Chapter 02 – Human Resource Department
description: An advanced quiz covering the establishment, structure, departmentalization bases, and staffing of the Human Resource Department.
subject: HRM1370 - Human Resource Management

questions.question: Which organizational scenario is LEAST likely to necessitate the establishment of a separate Human Resource Department (HRD), assuming all other factors are constant?
options: A highly complex, multi-site operation with diverse regulatory requirements.
A medium-scale firm experiencing a high degree of line manager turnover due to overwhelming administrative duties.
A small business where the owner can manage all HRM functions with minimal time consumption.
A large organization that requires maximum consistency and expertise in its labor relations decisions.
questions.answer: A small business where the owner can manage all HRM functions with minimal time consumption.
questions.rationale: One of the reasons to establish a separate HRD is when HRM functions become too time consuming or complex for line managers; a small business with minimal time consumption would have less need.

questions.question: In a large-scale organization, the head of the HR Department is typically titled AGM (HRM). What does the 'Top' level status of this role primarily imply regarding its organizational function?
options: The role is responsible only for transactional support jobs like typing and reception.
The role possesses Line Authority to directly give orders to non-HR department employees.
The role is an HR Executive position that links top management with HR specialists and generalists.
The role operates as an HR Generalist handling all functions within a specific plant or unit.
questions.answer: The role is an HR Executive position that links top management with HR specialists and generalists.
questions.rationale: HR Executive positions, like AGM (HRM), are at the top level and serve as the link between top management and the HR specialists and generalists.

questions.question: The 'Field Base' of departmentalization within the HRD organizes sub-departments based on which principle?
options: The type of employee group served (e.g., managers, clerical staff).
The specific HRM function or field (e.g., Rewards Management, Employee Resourcing).
The distinct business units or product lines of the organization.
The geographical location of the organization's factories.
questions.answer: The specific HRM function or field (e.g., Rewards Management, Employee Resourcing).
questions.rationale: Field Base departmentalization establishes sub-departments within HR according to fields of HRM, such as Rewards Management, Employee Resourcing, HR Development, and Labour Relations.

questions.question: A line manager decides to dismiss an employee but must first secure approval from the HR Manager before the decision can be implemented. Which type of authority has been delegated to the HR Manager in this situation?
questions.rationale: Concurrent Authority means the manager cannot take an HR decision without getting the approval of the HR manager.

questions.question: An HR Manager who is a HR Generalist in a small organization or a plant of a larger firm, handling most or all HRM functions, is classified under which job category according to Stone & Meltz (1993)?
questions.rationale: Operating HR managers are HR generalists who handle most or all HRM functions in a small organization or a plant of a larger firm.

questions.question: A multinational corporation structures its HR department with separate HR Managers for its North American Business Unit, European Business Unit, and Asian Business Unit. This is an application of which departmentalization base?
questions.rationale: The Business Base departmentalization organizes HR managers by distinct business units (e.g., Business A, B, C, D).

questions.question: Which title for an HR head is historically associated with the earliest period (Around 1900-1917) of the evolution of the HR Manager role?
questions.rationale: According to the timeline, 'Welfare officers' were the job title for the period Around 1900-1917.

questions.question: If the Head of HR in an organization is titled 'Personnel Administrator,' what is the most likely size and corresponding organizational status of the HR head, according to the provided structures?
questions.rationale: The summary table indicates that a 'Personnel Administrator' is the title for the HR head in a small organization, corresponding to a lower-level status.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key characteristic that distinguishes an HR Specialist from an HR Generalist?
options: An HR Specialist handles most or all HRM functions in a small organization.
An HR Specialist is employed only in small and medium organizations.
An HR Specialist is competent in several functions across the HR domain.
An HR Specialist has in-depth knowledge and expertise in a limited area of HR.
questions.answer: An HR Specialist has in-depth knowledge and expertise in a limited area of HR.
questions.rationale: An HR Specialist is defined as a person with in-depth knowledge and expertise in a limited area of HR, contrasting with the Generalist's competence in several functions.

questions.question: The fundamental justification for establishing a separate Human Resource Department is realized when:
options: The department operates at a lower cost than the financial benefits it generates.
The organization reaches a minimum employee threshold of 50 people.
The HR manager is granted Functional Authority over all line managers.
The complexity of line managers' jobs is reduced, even if costs exceed benefits.
questions.answer: The department operates at a lower cost than the financial benefits it generates.
questions.rationale: A separate department should be established when its benefits exceed the cost of establishing it.

questions.question: In a large-scale organization structure, the HR functions are typically divided into main managerial sections such as Planning & Staffing, HR Development, Rewards, and Labour Relations. Which base of departmentalization does this structure most closely represent at the managerial level?
questions.rationale: The Field Base departmentalization organizes sub-departments based on fields of HRM, and the structure of a large-scale HRD uses major HRM fields (like Planning & Staffing, HR Development, Rewards, Labour Relations) as its primary sub-department divisions.

questions.question: Which factor influencing the organization of the HR Department is fundamentally dependent on the organization's financial success and operational efficiency?
questions.rationale: Profitability directly relates to financial success and efficiency, making it the most appropriate choice compared to the other factors listed.

questions.question: An HR Manager who is a 'Staff Manager' primarily contributes to the organization by:
options: Giving direct orders to employees in other functional areas (e.g., Marketing, Finance).
Providing HR services, advice, and recommendations to line managers.
Replacing the CEO in making strategic business decisions.
Taking responsibility for the profit and loss of a specific business unit.
questions.answer: Providing HR services, advice, and recommendations to line managers.
questions.rationale: An HR manager is a staff manager who does not have Line Authority (give orders) but has staff authority (give advice, recommendations, suggestions) and provides HR services to line and other staff managers.

questions.question: In the 'Mixed Base' departmentalization structure, which of the following is NOT necessarily included, but instead represents a single, narrow specialization within the Field Base component?
options: Division by HR Functional Fields (Field Base)
questions.rationale: The Mixed Base is a combination of Field, Functional, Employee, Business, and Factory Bases. While the 'Employee Base' as a whole is an option, 'Division by Top/Middle Management' is a specific sub-division within the Employee Base, making it the more specific component rather than a primary basis of the mixed model itself. The structure shown for Mixed Base combines Business Base with Field Base and then sub-divides the Field Base by Employee type, indicating all are primary components of the mixture.

questions.question: Which type of authority represents the most powerful delegation to an HR manager, allowing them to issue binding orders instead of just advice in certain areas?
questions.rationale: Functional Authority is the most powerful authority, allowing the HR manager to give orders instead of advice, representing expanded HR manager authority.

questions.question: A key reason for establishing a separate HRD is the need for [dropdown_1] in HRM decisions, while an influential factor in determining its organization is the organization's [dropdown_2].
questions.rationale: Need for consistency in HRM decisions is a reason for a separate department. Financial strength is a factor influencing the organization of the HR department.

questions.question: The 'Personnel Manager' title was prevalent during the period of [dropdown_1], reflecting a historical shift from the earlier 'Labor manager' title, which was in use from [dropdown_2] onwards.
questions.rationale: The Personnel Manager title was used from 1960-1980. The Labor Manager title was used from 1918-1939.

questions.question: An HR Manager focused exclusively on 'Recruitment and Selection' is an example of an HR [dropdown_1], while an HR Manager responsible for all functions at 'Factory A' is categorized as an HR [dropdown_2].
questions.rationale: A focus on a limited area (Recruitment and Selection) indicates an HR Specialist. An HR Manager handling all functions in a plant (Factory HR Manager) is an example of an HR Generalist.

questions.question: Departmentalization in the HRD based on distinct organizational product lines or services is known as the __________ Base.
questions.rationale: The Business Base of departmentalization establishes HR managers for specific business units or product lines (Business A, B, C, etc.).

questions.question: The type of authority that makes it compulsory for a line manager to consult the HR manager before making any HRM decisions about their subordinates is called __________.
questions.rationale: Compulsory Staff Consultation requires the line manager to consult with the HR manager before making relevant HRM decisions.

questions.question: According to Stone and Meltz (1993), jobs like Typist, Receptionist, and Clerk within the HRD are classified as __________ jobs.
questions.rationale: Support jobs are classified as Typist, Receptionist, and Clerk roles within the HRD.

questions.question: Select all reasons for an organization to establish a separate Human Resource Department.
options: Need for expertise in HR fields.
Reduction of external influences to zero.
Need for consistency in HRM decisions.
answer: Need for expertise in HR fields.
Need for consistency in HRM decisions.
questions.rationale: Reasons for a separate HRD include: Need for expertise, Complexity of line managers' job, Coordination of HRM functions, Need for consistency in HRM decisions, External influences, and Time consuming. Reduction of external influences to zero is an unrealistic outcome, not a stated reason.

questions.question: Which of the following organizational characteristics are typically considered factors when determining the structure and organization of the HR Department?
questions.rationale: Factors to consider when organizing the HR Department include Nature of the organization, Size, Top management philosophy, Financial strength, Profitability, and Geographical location. Competitor's market share is not listed as a factor.

questions.question: Which of the following HR Manager roles would be classified as a 'Technical and professional job' (Functional specialist in specific areas) according to Stone & Meltz's classification?
questions.rationale: Technical and professional jobs are functional specialists in specific areas, such as Labour Relation, Reward, or Training Managers. Typist is a Support job.

questions.question: Match the size of the organization with the corresponding status (level of the head) of its HR department.
Match the departmentalization base with the primary characteristic used for sub-dividing the HR department.

matchPairs.right: Type of employee group (e.g., Clerical, Technical)
Specific HR function (e.g., Pay, T&D, Performance Evaluation)
Geographical or operational production unit (e.g., Factory A, B)

questions.question: Match the historical period with the dominant job title used for the HR role.

If an HR department is structured where an AGM - HRM oversees HR Managers for Employee Resourcing, HR Development, Rewards Management, and Labour Relations, this organizational design is a pure example of which departmentalization base?
options: Mixed Base, combining various organizational criteria.
Field Base, dividing by major HRM fields.
Business Base, focusing on distinct profit centers.
questions.answer: Field Base, dividing by major HRM fields.
questions.rationale: This structure exactly matches the description of the Field Base departmentalization, which establishes sub-departments based on the major fields of HRM.

questions.question: What is the primary role difference between an HR Generalist and an HR Specialist, in terms of expertise?
options: Generalists work only in large firms; Specialists work only in small firms.
Generalists need education in one field; Specialists need it in all HR functions.
Generalists are competent in several HR functions; Specialists are competent in one particular area.
Generalists are staff managers; Specialists have line authority.
questions.answer: Generalists are competent in several HR functions; Specialists are competent in one particular area.
questions.rationale: An HR Generalist is competent in several functions, having responsibility for a variety of HR activities. An HR Specialist is competent in one particular area and has in-depth knowledge in a limited area.

questions.question: The evolution of the HR Manager's title from 'Welfare officers' (Around 1900-1917) to 'Human Capital/People Manager/Talent Manager' (2020 up to date) primarily reflects a shift towards:
options: A focus on purely administrative and transactional tasks.
A decline in the authority delegated to the HR function.
A more strategic, comprehensive, and value-focused role within the organization.
A limitation of HR responsibilities solely to labor union negotiations.
questions.answer: A more strategic, comprehensive, and value-focused role within the organization.
questions.rationale: The progression of titles (Welfare, Labor, Personnel, HR, Human Capital/Talent) reflects an increasing scope and strategic importance of the role, moving from basic welfare/transactional tasks to managing human resources as critical capital/talent.
